revision.comment: creating article from translation done at [[User:HelloAnnyong/Translated/Shuin]]
revision.text:
{{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} [[Image:大蓮寺.jpg|right|thumb|A shuin from Dairen-ji]] A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|朱印}} is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[Shinto shrine]]s and Buddhist temples in [[Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|shuinchō|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=4056064512|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&lpg=PA50&dq=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50#v=onepage&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&f=false}}</ref>
== Summary == The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the temples, monks, or [[kannushi]]. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write the name of the temple, the day of the visit, and other messages on and around the stamped portions.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[Shakyō|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money to a shrine, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=4569669301|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&lpg=PA1&dq=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1#v=onepage&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&f=false}}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|accessdate=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi}}</ref> At [[Itsukushima Shrine]], they have a sign that asks people to dedicate their feelings.
Special shuinchō (and occasioanlly hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|accessdate=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==References== {{reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Shuin}} [[Category:Temples in Japan]]
[[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]]

{{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} [[Image:大蓮寺.jpg|right|thumb|A shuin from Dairen-ji]] A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|朱印}} is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[Shinto shrine]]s and Buddhist temples in [[Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|shuinchō|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=4056064512|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&lpg=PA50&dq=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50#v=onepage&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&f=false}}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the temples, monks, or [[kannushi]]. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write the name of the temple, the day of the visit, and other messages on and around the stamped portions.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[Shakyō|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money to a shrine, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=4569669301|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&lpg=PA1&dq=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1#v=onepage&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&f=false}}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|accessdate=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi}}</ref> At [[Itsukushima Shrine]], they have a sign that asks people to dedicate their feelings.
Special shuinchō (and occasioanlly hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|accessdate=24 December 2012}}</ref>

{{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} [[Image:大蓮寺.jpg|right|thumb|A shuin from Dairen-ji]] A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|朱印}} is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[Shinto shrine]]s and Buddhist temples in [[Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=4056064512|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&lpg=PA50&dq=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50#v=onepage&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&f=false}}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the temples: Buddhist monks, or Shinto ''[[kannushi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in his [[Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the name of the temple, the day of the visit, and other messages on and around the stamped portions.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[Shakyō|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=4569669301|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&lpg=PA1&dq=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1#v=onepage&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&f=false}}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|accessdate=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi}}</ref> At [[Itsukushima Shrine]], there is a sign that asks people to dedicate their feelings.
Special shuinchō (and occasioanlly hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|accessdate=24 December 2012}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in [[Zentsū-ji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

{{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in [[Zentsū-ji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

{{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in [[Zentsū-ji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

{{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in [[Zentsū-ji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

{{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in [[Zentsū-ji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

{{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in [[Zentsū-ji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q7504772]] Wikidata name: shuin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s and [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[:en:Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[:en:Buddhist deities|Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[:en:Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>

People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>

As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7504772]] Wikidata name: shuin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s and [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[:en:Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[:en:Buddhist deities|Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[:en:Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>

Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>

It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7504772]] Wikidata name: shuin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s and [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[:en:Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[:en:Buddhist deities|Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[:en:Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>

A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s and [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[:en:Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[:en:Buddhist deities|Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[:en:Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7504772]] Wikidata name: shuin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s and [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[:en:Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[:en:Buddhist deities|Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[:en:Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>

{{wikidata link|Q7504772}} [[Category:Buddhist symbols]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Temples in Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7504772]] Wikidata name: shuin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s and [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[:en:Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[:en:Buddhist deities|Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[:en:Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>

It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>

A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s and [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist [[:en:Bhikkhu|Hōshi]]''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their [[:en:Japanese calligraphy|distinctive calligraphy]], the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or [[:en:Buddhist deities|Buddhist deities]], and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated [[:en:Sutra copying|copied sutra]]. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>

A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s and {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist {{ill|Bhikkhu|en|Bhikkhu|WD=Q854997|lt=Hōshi}}''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their {{ill|Japanese calligraphy|en|Japanese calligraphy|WD=Q660086|lt=distinctive calligraphy}}, the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or {{ill|Buddhist deities|en|Buddhist deities|WD=Q65122124|lt=Buddhist deities}}, and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated {{ill|Sutra copying|en|Sutra copying|WD=Q3462275|lt=copied sutra}}. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsū-ji}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa}} File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsū-ji}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa}} File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7504772]] Wikidata name: shuin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s and {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist {{ill|Bhikkhu|en|Bhikkhu|WD=Q854997|lt=Hōshi}}''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their {{ill|Japanese calligraphy|en|Japanese calligraphy|WD=Q660086|lt=distinctive calligraphy}}, the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or {{ill|Buddhist deities|en|Buddhist deities|WD=Q65122124|lt=Buddhist deities}}, and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated {{ill|Sutra copying|en|Sutra copying|WD=Q3462275|lt=copied sutra}}. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>

People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
