<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]]
{{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s and {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist {{ill|Bhikkhu|en|Bhikkhu|WD=Q854997|lt=Hōshi|1=Bhikkhu}}''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their {{ill|Japanese calligraphy|en|Japanese calligraphy|WD=Q660086|lt=distinctive calligraphy|1=Japanese calligraphy}}, the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or {{ill|Buddhist deities|en|Buddhist deities|WD=Q65122124|lt=Buddhist deities|1=Buddhist deities}}, and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated {{ill|Sutra copying|en|Sutra copying|WD=Q3462275|lt=copied sutra|1=Sutra copying}}. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsū-ji|1=Zentsū-ji}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa|1=Kagawa Prefecture}} File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]]
{{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s and {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist {{ill|Bhikkhu|en|Bhikkhu|WD=Q854997|lt=Hōshi|1=Bhikkhu}}''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their {{ill|Japanese calligraphy|en|Japanese calligraphy|WD=Q660086|lt=distinctive calligraphy|1=Japanese calligraphy}}, the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or {{ill|Buddhist deities|en|Buddhist deities|WD=Q65122124|lt=Buddhist deities|1=Buddhist deities}}, and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated {{ill|Sutra copying|en|Sutra copying|WD=Q3462275|lt=copied sutra|1=Sutra copying}}. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsū-ji|1=Zentsū-ji}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa|1=Kagawa Prefecture}} File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Goshuin]][[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shuin]][[en:Shuin]][[fr:Goshuin]][[ja:朱印 (神社仏閣)]][[ms:Syuin]][[simple:Shuin]][[zh:御朱印]]
{{Short description|Seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{for|the Japanese merchant sailing ships|Red seal ships}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}} ---- {{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:Sikokuɵɵkakeziku.jpg|200px|thumb|A stampbook with tons of shuin]]}}
A {{nihongo|'''shuin'''|[[Wiktionary:朱印|朱印]]}}, also called "'''Go-shuin''' (御朱印)" as an [[Honorific_speech_in_Japanese|honorific]], is a seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s and {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The seal stamps are often collected in books called {{nihongo|''shuinchō''|朱印帳}} that are sold at shrines and temples.<ref name=powerspot>{{cite book|title=開運ご利益東京・下町散歩: スカイツリー周辺・下町・江戶パワースポット|year=2011|isbn=978-4056064513|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fWFRMYi6JggC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA50|author1=学研パブリッシング・編|publisher=学研パブリッシング }}</ref>
The stamps are different from commemorative stamps in that they are made by people who work at the shrines or temples: Shinto ''[[Kannushi]] or Buddhist {{ill|Bhikkhu|en|Bhikkhu|WD=Q854997|lt=Hōshi|1=Bhikkhu}}''. To create the shuin, the writer presses down one or more large stamps, and then uses black ink to write, in their {{ill|Japanese calligraphy|en|Japanese calligraphy|WD=Q660086|lt=distinctive calligraphy|1=Japanese calligraphy}}, the day of the visit, the name of the shrine or temple, as well as sometimes the names of the relevant [[Kami]] or {{ill|Buddhist deities|en|Buddhist deities|WD=Q65122124|lt=Buddhist deities|1=Buddhist deities}}, and other messages.
There are various theories about the origin of shuin, though the strongest is that it was a receipt for a dedicated {{ill|Sutra copying|en|Sutra copying|WD=Q3462275|lt=copied sutra|1=Sutra copying}}. There are still temples where one cannot receive a shuin without having donated a sutra or money, but the majority of the temples will now accept a small amount of money for a shuin. It usually costs 300 yen,<ref name=ichinomiya>{{cite book|title=全国「一の宮」徹底ガイド|year=2007|isbn=978-4569669304|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=72U6AkIGRacC&q=%E6%9C%B1%E5%8D%B0%E3%80%80%E3%81%8A%E5%AF%BA&pg=PA1|author1=恵美嘉樹|publisher=PHP研究所 }}</ref> though there are some places that charge up to 1000 yen.<ref name=chunichi>{{cite news|title=専用色紙に朱印もらおう 津で「七福神めぐり」|url=http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|access-date=24 December 2012|newspaper=Chunichi|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130221001620/http://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/mie/20121204/CK2012120402000024.html|archive-date=21 February 2013}}</ref>
As shuin are given at both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, there is some discussion as to whether stamps from both shrines and temples can be written in the same ''shuinchō''.<ref>[https://xn--wlrp7z7zf.jp/goshuin_sanpo/archives/84 "FAQ - Can I mix stamps from temples and shrines in one stamp book?"] retrieved May 31 2024 </ref> While ultimately a matter of personal preference, there are rare instances in which a shrine or temple may refuse to write a shuin if the ''shuinchō'' contains [[shinbutsu bunri|both Buddhist and Shinto stamps]].<ref>[http://ezojinjya.jp/s7arkr20gsi.html) "Before collecting the stamps at Ezogashima Shrine"] retrieved May 31 2024</ref>
Special ''shuinchō'' (and occasionally hanging scrolls) are available for people who do pilgrimages such as the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] and the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. People who do the Shikoku Pilgrimage can also get shuin on the white robes they sometimes wear.
A number of [[Jōdo Shinshū]] temples do not offer shuin, in order to discourage the practice of visiting for the sake of only collecting shuin.<ref>{{cite web|title=朱印をしない理由|url=http://www.higashihonganji.or.jp/sermon/leaflet/02.html|access-date=24 December 2012}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Saiɡokuɜɜ0ɨ.jpg|shuinchō (朱印帳) File:Shuin being made in Zentsuji - Kagawa - 2021 April 1.webm|Shuin being made in {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsū-ji|1=Zentsū-ji}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa|1=Kagawa Prefecture}} File:Noukyoutyo.jpg|kasane-in (重ね印) File:Byakue.jpg|byakue (白衣) File:Omie01a.jpg|miei (御影) File:Kinensanɡe.jpg|sange (散華) File:写真.jpg|mangan (満願の証) File:大蓮寺.jpg|A shuin from Dairen-ji (大蓮寺) File:Tohorenɡyou.jpg|pilgrimage </gallery>
